behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is real filesystem watching (recursive watch behavior across directory levels), which depends on OS event delivery and watcher-library interaction. A mocked watcher can hide misconfiguration that only appears with real directory events. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified because the behavior contains no AI/LLM decision step.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon against a real temp watched directory. Create a nested subdirectory and write a file there. Assert end-to-end that exactly one transformed output is produced for that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: The behavior text is about deduplication logic itself (same path + time window), which can be exercised as deterministic in-memory logic with a fake clock. No real OS/network/subprocess is required for this specific failure mode. Higher levels are not required unless testing watcher/event wiring, which this behavior does not specify.
behaviors.test_description: Test the dedup component/function directly: submit the same normalized path twice within the configured window and assert second request is rejected; then advance fake time past window and assert it is accepted.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected Integration because behavior targets logic, not module handoff; rejected System because no real OS primitive is needed.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly depends on OS duplicate event emission. This is a real infrastructure condition; mocked events often miss timing/coalescing quirks that cause duplicate processing in production. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified (no AI/LLM output dependency).
behaviors.test_description: With the daemon running on a real watched directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., write+rename or editor-style temp-file swap). Assert only one pipeline execution/output occurs for that logical file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is an OS-level directory lifecycle scenario (delete/recreate inode/path) and requires real watcher re-subscription behavior under live filesystem changes. In-process mocks can falsely pass by skipping actual watch invalidation semantics. No LLM or multi-agent chain is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real directory. Delete the watched directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file. Assert the daemon processes the new file without process restart.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real filesystem timestamp semantics (creation/birth time resolution) and real concurrent arrival behavior from the watcher/OS. Integration tests with synthetic events can miss ordering failures caused by actual event timing and metadata behavior. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by lack of AI-dependent decisions.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files nearly simultaneously in the watched directory using real filesystem operations, with controlled creation order. Assert processing order follows filesystem creation timestamps rather than raw event arrival order.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected Integration because the behavior’s risk is real timestamp/event behavior at OS boundary, not only queue sorting in isolation.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a classic real filesystem race (event before write completion) tied to OS write buffering and event timing. Mocks typically hide partial-write states, so they can pass while production fails. No AI model output dependency, so Agentic/Workflow do not apply.
behaviors.test_description: Use a real watched directory and write a large file in chunks with delays to keep it incomplete after initial arrival event. Assert daemon defers processing until final write/close and then processes once with complete content.
